results.title: WEAVER Definition & Meaning - Merriam-Webster
results.content: 1. One that weaves especially as an occupation. 2. Any of numerous Old World passerine birds (family Ploceidae) that resemble finches and typically construct

results.content:

Appearance

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Wiktionary logo

Look up weaver in Wiktionary, the free dictionary.

Weaver or Weavers may refer to:

## Activities

[edit]

 A person who engages in weaving fabric

## Animals

[edit]

 Various birds of the family Ploceidae
 Crevice weaver spider family
 Orb-weaver spider family
 Weever (or weever-fish)

## Arts and entertainment

[edit] [...] Weaver Marquez, a character in the narrative videogame Kentucky Route Zero
 Grigori Weaver, a character in the 2010 video game Call of Duty: Black Ops and the 2020 sequel Call of Duty: Black Ops Cold War
 The Weavers, a race of intelligent spiders in Hollow Knight and Hollow Knight: Silksong [...] ## Other uses

[edit]

 Weaver (surname) "Weaver (surname)")
 Weavers (surname) "Weavers (surname)")
 Weaver line, a railway line in London
 Weaver Network, a planned public transport network in West Yorkshire
 Weaver rail mount, a style of mount used to attach a scope to a firearm or crossbow
 Weaver stance, a two-handed stance for use when firing handguns
 Dokumacılar (English: Weavers), a terrorist organisation part of the Islamic State of Iraq and the Levant active in Turkey

## See also

[edit]

Search for "weaver" on Wikipedia.

results.title: WEAVER Definition & Meaning | Dictionary.com
results.content:
## Explanation

Vocabulary.com

A person who makes fabric by weaving fiber together is a weaver. Most weavers use a loom, a device that holds the threads tightly as they're being woven. A craft weaver works by hand, weaving without a loom, but most weavers use either a hand loom or a power loom. This more mechanized type of loom was invented in the 1780s, and it made the work less physically taxing for weavers. The Proto-Indo-European root of weaver is also the source of the Sanskrit word ubhnati, "he laces together" and the Greek word hyphe, or "web."

## Vocabulary lists containing weaver

Vocabulary.com

### Name That Job: Occupational Last Names [...] # weaver

## noun

a person who weaves.

a person whose occupation is weaving.

a weaverbird.

## noun

James Baird, 1833–1912, U.S. politician: congressman 1879–81, 1885–89.

Robert Clifton, 1907–97, U.S. economist and government official: first Secretary of Housing and Urban Development, 1966–68.

## noun

a person who weaves, esp as a means of livelihood

short for weaverbird

## Etymology

### Origin of weaver

First recorded in 1325–75, weaver is from the Middle English word wevere. See weave, -er 1

## Explanation

results.content:
Vocabulary.com

# weaver

Other forms: weavers

A person who makes fabric by weaving fiber together is a weaver. Most weavers use a loom, a device that holds the threads tightly as they're being woven.

A craft weaver works by hand, weaving without a loom, but most weavers use either a hand loom or a power loom. This more mechanized type of loom was invented in the 1780s, and it made the work less physically taxing for weavers. The Proto-Indo-European root of weaver is also the source of the Sanskrit word ubhnati, "he laces together" and the Greek word hyphe, or "web."

results.title: weaver, n.¹ meanings, etymology and more | Oxford English Dictionary
results.content:
### What is the earliest known use of the noun weaver?

Earliest known use

Middle English

The earliest known use of the noun weaver is in the Middle English period (1150—1500).

OED's earliest evidence for weaver is from 1362, in the writing of William Langland, poet.

### How is the noun weaver pronounced?

#### British English

Listen to pronunciation

#### U.S. English
